1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the law of the Nazarite now given, set after the law of the suspected wife. The witnesses note that Gill notes the connection some Jewish writers draw — that since wine leads to adultery, the Nazarite's abstinence from it and closer devotion to God were means of preserving from unchastity. The witnesses note that the vow concerned women as well as men. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the after the jealousy law — set following the law of the suspected wife; the wine and unchastity — abstinence a guard against the sins of the flesh; the fuller consecration — the way of those who draw nearer to God.

The witnesses agree the placing is fitting: the previous law dealt with the breaking of marriage, and here follows the way of fuller consecration, the Nazarite vowing a stricter separation to God; the very discipline that guards against the sins of the flesh — abstinence, restraint, and devotion — is set forth as the path of those who would draw nearer to the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of the Nazarite: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the after the jealousy law: set following the law of the suspected wife. The witnesses note the wine and unchastity: abstinence a guard against the sins of the flesh. The witnesses note the fuller consecration: the way of those who draw nearer to God. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the after the jealousy law: set following the law of the suspected wife. The expositors observe the wine and unchastity: abstinence a guard against the sins of the flesh. The expositors observe the fuller consecration: the way of those who draw nearer to God. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the after the jealousy law, the wine and unchastity, the fuller consecration, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that God gave the law of the Nazarite after the law of jealousy — the discipline of consecration set forth as the path of those who would draw nearer to the LORD.
2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When either man or woman shall separate themselves to vow a vow of a Nazarite, to separate themselves unto the LORD:" The witnesses note the vow open to man or woman alike — a voluntary separation unto the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jewish canon that Gentiles have no Nazariteship, the law binding only the children of Israel, though Gentiles might bring vows and freewill offerings. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read it of man or woman separating themselves unto the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the man or woman — the vow open to either; the to vow a vow — a freewill act of devotion; the to separate unto the LORD — a stricter holiness for a season; the for the willing — a higher consecration, not a duty on all.

The witnesses agree the Nazarite vow was a freewill act of devotion open to every Israelite, woman as well as man: any who chose might separate themselves to a stricter holiness for a season; the vow was not a duty laid on all but a higher consecration offered to the willing — a path of nearer devotion for those whose hearts moved them to give themselves more fully to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the voluntary vow: The witnesses note the man or woman: the vow open to either. The witnesses note the to vow a vow: a freewill act of devotion. The witnesses note the to separate unto the LORD: a stricter holiness for a season. The witnesses note the for the willing: a higher consecration, not a duty on all. The expositors observe the man or woman: the vow open to either. The expositors observe the to vow a vow: a freewill act of devotion. The expositors observe the to separate unto the LORD: a stricter holiness for a season. The expositors observe the for the willing: a higher consecration, not a duty on all. The believer is taught to mark the man or woman, the to vow a vow, the to separate unto the LORD, the for the willing, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that man or woman alike might vow the Nazarite separation — a freewill consecration offered to the willing, a path of nearer devotion to God.
3:
"He shall separate himself from wine and strong drink, and shall drink no vinegar of wine, or vinegar of strong drink, neither shall he drink any liquor of grapes..." The witnesses note the first mark of the Nazarite — abstinence from wine and all strong drink. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was not from excess only but from drinking wine at all, that he might be more free and fit for the service of God, less liable to temptation. The witnesses note that Aben Ezra observed many transgressions are occasioned by wine, which intoxicates the mind and unfits for religious duties. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from wine and strong drink — the first mark of the vow; the not from excess only — but from drinking wine at all; the for clearer devotion — that the mind be free for God's service; the against temptation — kept from the lusts wine excites.

The witnesses agree the abstinence was for the sake of devotion, not for its own merit: the Nazarite gave up even the lawful use of wine that his mind might be clear for prayer, meditation, and the worship of God, and that he be kept from the lusts and follies that strong drink excites; the renunciation of a good thing for a season set him apart for a closer walk with the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse abstinence from wine: The witnesses note the from wine and strong drink: the first mark of the vow. The witnesses note the not from excess only: but from drinking wine at all. The witnesses note the for clearer devotion: that the mind be free for God's service. The witnesses note the against temptation: kept from the lusts wine excites. The expositors observe the from wine and strong drink: the first mark of the vow. The expositors observe the not from excess only: but from drinking wine at all. The expositors observe the for clearer devotion: that the mind be free for God's service. The expositors observe the against temptation: kept from the lusts wine excites. The believer is taught to mark the from wine and strong drink, the not from excess only, the for clearer devotion, the against temptation, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite abstained from wine and strong drink — a good thing renounced for a season, that his mind be clear for God and kept from temptation.
4:
"All the days of his separation shall he eat nothing that is made of the vine tree, from the kernels even to the husk." The witnesses note the abstinence extended to the whole vine — nothing from kernel to husk to be eaten. The witnesses note that Gill notes it covered the leaves, branches, and fruit, in any food, liquor, or sauce. The witnesses note that the Jews held the vow, if not specified, to last thirty days at least. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made of the vine tree — no food or drink of the grape; the from kernel to husk — down to the smallest part; the the whole vine — leaves, branches, and fruit alike; the a complete renunciation — consecration holding back nothing.

The witnesses agree the separation from the vine was complete and exacting: not the wine only but every product of the grape, down to the smallest part, was forbidden the Nazarite; the thoroughness of the abstinence taught that consecration is not a half-measure but a whole renunciation in its appointed sphere — the one who is set apart to God holding back nothing of what his vow embraced.

So the witnesses make the verse nothing of the vine: The witnesses note the made of the vine tree: no food or drink of the grape. The witnesses note the from kernel to husk: down to the smallest part. The witnesses note the the whole vine: leaves, branches, and fruit alike. The witnesses note the a complete renunciation: consecration holding back nothing. The expositors observe the made of the vine tree: no food or drink of the grape. The expositors observe the from kernel to husk: down to the smallest part. The expositors observe the the whole vine: leaves, branches, and fruit alike. The expositors observe the a complete renunciation: consecration holding back nothing. The believer is taught to mark the made of the vine tree, the from kernel to husk, the the whole vine, the a complete renunciation, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite ate nothing of the vine, from kernel to husk — the thoroughness of the abstinence teaching that consecration is a whole renunciation, holding back nothing.
5:
"All the days of the vow of his separation there shall no razor come upon his head... he shall let the locks of the hair of his head grow." The witnesses note the second mark of the Nazarite — the unshorn hair, no razor coming upon his head. The witnesses note that Gill notes the vow might be for a week, a month, or more, but not less than thirty days. The witnesses note that Clarke sums the Nazarite vow — a special consecration to God, for a season, the hair left to grow to be burned at last under the peace offering (v.18). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no razor — the hair left untouched; the let the locks grow — the long hair worn as a sign; the the badge of the vow — the visible mark of separation; the a crown of consecration — declaring the Nazarite wholly God's.

The witnesses agree the long hair was the visible badge of the vow: while others trimmed and dressed their hair, the Nazarite let it grow untouched as the open sign of his separation, a crown of consecration worn upon the head; the outward mark, borne for all to see, declared that this man or woman belonged for the time wholly to God, set apart by a visible token of devotion.

So the witnesses make the verse the unshorn hair: The witnesses note the no razor: the hair left untouched. The witnesses note the let the locks grow: the long hair worn as a sign. The witnesses note the the badge of the vow: the visible mark of separation. The witnesses note the a crown of consecration: declaring the Nazarite wholly God's. The expositors observe the no razor: the hair left untouched. The expositors observe the let the locks grow: the long hair worn as a sign. The expositors observe the the badge of the vow: the visible mark of separation. The expositors observe the a crown of consecration: declaring the Nazarite wholly God's. The believer is taught to mark the no razor, the let the locks grow, the the badge of the vow, the a crown of consecration, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite let his hair grow unshorn — the visible badge of the vow, a crown of consecration declaring him wholly God's for the season.
6:
"All the days that he separateth himself unto the LORD he shall come at no dead body." The witnesses note the third mark — the Nazarite was to approach no dead body all the days of his vow. The witnesses note that Gill notes the phrase "all the days that he separateth himself unto the LORD" is repeated at each new branch of the law, showing each part strictly to be kept. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch ground the rule — because he wore the diadem of his God on his head in the growth of his hair and was holy to the LORD, he was to come at no dead person. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come at no dead body — the Nazarite to avoid the dead; the the diadem on his head — the hair a crown of consecration; the holy to the LORD — separated to the living God; the like the high priest — kept clear of death's defilement.

The witnesses agree the avoidance of the dead marked the Nazarite's likeness to the holiest: the unshorn hair was a diadem of consecration, and as the high priest might not defile himself with the dead, so neither might the Nazarite during his vow; the one set apart to the living God was to keep clear of the chief sign of sin's curse, holiness and death not to meet upon his person.

So the witnesses make the verse no contact with the dead: The witnesses note the come at no dead body: the Nazarite to avoid the dead. The witnesses note the the diadem on his head: the hair a crown of consecration. The witnesses note the holy to the LORD: separated to the living God. The witnesses note the like the high priest: kept clear of death's defilement. The expositors observe the come at no dead body: the Nazarite to avoid the dead. The expositors observe the the diadem on his head: the hair a crown of consecration. The expositors observe the holy to the LORD: separated to the living God. The expositors observe the like the high priest: kept clear of death's defilement. The believer is taught to mark the come at no dead body, the the diadem on his head, the holy to the LORD, the like the high priest, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite came at no dead body — the one consecrated to the living God kept clear of death's defilement, like the high priest in his holiness.
7:
"He shall not make himself unclean for his father, or for his mother, for his brother, or for his sister, when they die: because the consecration of his God is upon his head." The witnesses note the strictness of the rule — the Nazarite not to defile himself even for his nearest kin, as the high priest might not. The witnesses note that Gill notes that what even the common priests might do — mourn and tend their dead kin — the Nazarite might not, being in this on a level with the high priest, which shows how sacred these persons were. The witnesses note that Gill draws the spiritual lesson for the Nazarites of grace to abstain from what would defile. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not for father or mother — no defiling even for nearest kin; the above the common priest — on a level with the high priest; the the consecration on his head — god's claim set above all; the the spiritual lesson — holding nothing dear above devotion to God.

The witnesses agree the bar against mourning even father or mother measured the height of the Nazarite's consecration: his vow set the claim of God above the dearest natural ties, so that not even a parent's death might draw him into defilement; Gill bids spiritual Nazarites learn from it to hold nothing, however dear, above their consecration to the Lord, the crown of God being upon them.

So the witnesses make the verse above natural ties: The witnesses note the not for father or mother: no defiling even for nearest kin. The witnesses note the above the common priest: on a level with the high priest. The witnesses note the the consecration on his head: God's claim set above all. The witnesses note the the spiritual lesson: holding nothing dear above devotion to God. The expositors observe the not for father or mother: no defiling even for nearest kin. The expositors observe the above the common priest: on a level with the high priest. The expositors observe the the consecration on his head: God's claim set above all. The expositors observe the the spiritual lesson: holding nothing dear above devotion to God. The believer is taught to mark the not for father or mother, the above the common priest, the the consecration on his head, the the spiritual lesson, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite might not defile himself even for father or mother — his consecration setting God's claim above the dearest ties, a lesson to hold nothing above devotion to the Lord.
8:
"All the days of his separation he is holy unto the LORD." The witnesses note the sum of the vow — all the days of his separation the Nazarite is holy unto the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill gathers the whole: set apart for God's service, separate from all others and especially the dead, bound to abstain from wine and shaving and defilement, and to be employed in holy exercises. The witnesses note that the holiness of the Nazarite lasted the whole term of the vow. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the days — the whole term of the vow; the he is holy — set apart wholly to the LORD; the the marks of holiness — the abstinences serving consecration; the days of nearness — the separation a drawing near to God.

The witnesses agree this verse is the heart of the matter: the Nazarite's abstinences were not ends in themselves but the marks of a holiness, a being set apart wholly to the LORD for the season of the vow; every restraint served the one purpose of consecration, that the days of separation be days of nearness to God — a figure of the believer's call to be holy, set apart to God in heart and life.

So the witnesses make the verse holy unto the LORD: The witnesses note the all the days: the whole term of the vow. The witnesses note the he is holy: set apart wholly to the LORD. The witnesses note the the marks of holiness: the abstinences serving consecration. The witnesses note the days of nearness: the separation a drawing near to God. The expositors observe the all the days: the whole term of the vow. The expositors observe the he is holy: set apart wholly to the LORD. The expositors observe the the marks of holiness: the abstinences serving consecration. The expositors observe the days of nearness: the separation a drawing near to God. The believer is taught to mark the all the days, the he is holy, the the marks of holiness, the days of nearness, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that all the days of his separation the Nazarite is holy unto the LORD — the abstinences serving one purpose, that the days be days of nearness and consecration to God.
9:
"And if any man die very suddenly by him, and he hath defiled the head of his consecration; then he shall shave his head in the day of his cleansing, on the seventh day shall he shave it." The witnesses note the case of sudden defilement — a man dying unexpectedly beside the Nazarite, polluting his consecrated head. The witnesses note that Gill notes the two words rendered "very suddenly," some taking them as synonymous and intensive, others (the Targums) as meaning a death by ignorance or chance, not wilful. The witnesses note that the defiled Nazarite was to shave his head on the seventh day, the day of his cleansing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a man die suddenly — an unforeseen death beside him; the the head defiled — the crown of consecration polluted; the shave the head — the consecrated hair removed; the the seventh day — the day of his cleansing.

The witnesses agree the law made provision for an undeserved defilement: a death the Nazarite could not foresee or prevent yet polluted the crown of his vow, and he must shave the consecrated hair and begin the cleansing; the case shows that even an involuntary touch of death broke the holiness of the vow, the consecration so tender that it could not abide the least contact with the curse.

So the witnesses make the verse the sudden defilement: The witnesses note the a man die suddenly: an unforeseen death beside him. The witnesses note the the head defiled: the crown of consecration polluted. The witnesses note the shave the head: the consecrated hair removed. The witnesses note the the seventh day: the day of his cleansing. The expositors observe the a man die suddenly: an unforeseen death beside him. The expositors observe the the head defiled: the crown of consecration polluted. The expositors observe the shave the head: the consecrated hair removed. The expositors observe the the seventh day: the day of his cleansing. The believer is taught to mark the a man die suddenly, the the head defiled, the shave the head, the the seventh day, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that an unforeseen death defiled the Nazarite's consecrated head, and he must shave it — the consecration so tender it could not abide the least contact with the curse.
10:
"And on the eighth day he shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons, to the priest, to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation:" The witnesses note the offering of the defiled Nazarite — two turtledoves or two young pigeons brought on the eighth day. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the offering of the poorer sort, as of childbearing women and those with an issue, the defilement requiring a sacrifice to purge it. The witnesses note that Gill sees it typical of the sacrifice of Christ, by which sin — even the sin cleaving to holy things — is put away. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the eighth day — the day appointed for the offering; the two turtles or pigeons — the offering of the poorer sort; the to purge defilement — the pollution removed only by sacrifice; the typical of Christ — his blood cleansing even the sins of holy things (1 Pet. 1:19).

The witnesses agree even an involuntary defilement could be removed only by blood: the Nazarite's pollution, though no fault of his own, required a sin offering, for nothing unclean might stand before God uncovered; Gill reads the doves as a figure of Christ's sacrifice, by which the very sins of our holy things are atoned, the believer's best service needing the cleansing of the blood (cf. 1 Pet. 1:19).

So the witnesses make the verse the offering of cleansing: The witnesses note the the eighth day: the day appointed for the offering. The witnesses note the two turtles or pigeons: the offering of the poorer sort. The witnesses note the to purge defilement: the pollution removed only by sacrifice. The witnesses note the typical of Christ: his blood cleansing even the sins of holy things (1 Pet. 1:19). The expositors observe the the eighth day: the day appointed for the offering. The expositors observe the two turtles or pigeons: the offering of the poorer sort. The expositors observe the to purge defilement: the pollution removed only by sacrifice. The expositors observe the typical of Christ: his blood cleansing even the sins of holy things (1 Pet. 1:19). The believer is taught to mark the the eighth day, the two turtles or pigeons, the to purge defilement, the typical of Christ, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the defiled Nazarite brought two doves to be offered — even involuntary defilement removed only by blood, a figure of Christ's sacrifice cleansing the sins of our holy things.
11:
"And the priest shall offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering, and make an atonement for him, for that he sinned by the dead, and shall hallow his head that same day." The witnesses note the two birds offered — one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering — to atone for the Nazarite and hallow his head afresh. The witnesses note that Gill notes both were necessary, the one to expiate sin, the other a gift to God in thankfulness for the acceptance of the first. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "he sinned by the dead" as on account of the dead, the contact reckoned a sin to be atoned. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a sin offering — to expiate the defilement; the a burnt offering — the gift of renewed devotion; the atonement made — the guilt of the dead put away; the the head hallowed — the consecration restored that day.

The witnesses agree the double offering shows the full pattern of restoration: first the sin offering to put away the guilt of the defilement, then the burnt offering of entire devotion renewed, and so the head hallowed again; the Nazarite, having lost his consecration by an unclean touch, was not cast off but cleansed and restored — a figure of the believer who, defiled, is purged by sacrifice and set apart to God afresh.

So the witnesses make the verse atonement and renewal: The witnesses note the a sin offering: to expiate the defilement. The witnesses note the a burnt offering: the gift of renewed devotion. The witnesses note the atonement made: the guilt of the dead put away. The witnesses note the the head hallowed: the consecration restored that day. The expositors observe the a sin offering: to expiate the defilement. The expositors observe the a burnt offering: the gift of renewed devotion. The expositors observe the atonement made: the guilt of the dead put away. The expositors observe the the head hallowed: the consecration restored that day. The believer is taught to mark the a sin offering, the a burnt offering, the atonement made, the the head hallowed, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that one bird was offered for sin and one for a burnt offering, atoning for the Nazarite and hallowing his head afresh — the defiled cleansed and set apart to God anew.
12:
"And he shall consecrate unto the LORD the days of his separation, and shall bring a lamb of the first year for a trespass offering: but the days that were before shall be lost, because his separation was defiled." The witnesses note that the Nazarite must begin his vow afresh — the former days lost, because his separation was defiled. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was to begin his account again from the shaving of his head, devoting as many days as he had vowed before. The witnesses note that Wesley reads "lost" as the days falling to the ground, void and of none effect. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the consecrate the days afresh — beginning the vow anew; the a lamb for trespass — the offering for the broken vow; the the days before lost — the former term counting for nothing; the begun, not resumed — holiness recovered wholly, not patched.

The witnesses agree the defilement cancelled all that had gone before: however many days of the vow were already kept, they counted for nothing once the consecration was broken, and the whole term must be begun anew; the sobering lesson is that a broken consecration is not patched but restarted, the days defiled falling void — a warning that holiness once forfeited must be wholly recovered, not merely resumed where it left off.

So the witnesses make the verse the lost days: The witnesses note the consecrate the days afresh: beginning the vow anew. The witnesses note the a lamb for trespass: the offering for the broken vow. The witnesses note the the days before lost: the former term counting for nothing. The witnesses note the begun, not resumed: holiness recovered wholly, not patched. The expositors observe the consecrate the days afresh: beginning the vow anew. The expositors observe the a lamb for trespass: the offering for the broken vow. The expositors observe the the days before lost: the former term counting for nothing. The expositors observe the begun, not resumed: holiness recovered wholly, not patched. The believer is taught to mark the consecrate the days afresh, the a lamb for trespass, the the days before lost, the begun, not resumed, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite began his vow afresh, the former days lost — a broken consecration not patched but wholly restarted, holiness once forfeited to be wholly recovered.
13:
"And this is the law of the Nazarite, when the days of his separation are fulfilled: he shall be brought unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation:" The witnesses note the law for the finishing of the vow — when the days are fulfilled, the Nazarite is brought to the door of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill notes this respects both what goes before (the abstinences and the case of defilement) and what follows (the offerings at the completion of the vow). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set this as the directions for the close of the vow. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the days fulfilled — the term of separation completed; the brought to the door — coming to the tabernacle to discharge it; the before the LORD — the vow ended as an act of worship; the thanksgiving — a sacrifice for grace to keep the vow.

The witnesses agree the completed vow was not ended in private but brought to the sanctuary: the Nazarite, his days fulfilled, came to the door of the tabernacle to discharge his vow with offerings before the LORD; the consecration that had been a personal devotion was concluded as a public act of worship, the willing servant rendering at the last a sacrifice of thanksgiving for grace to keep his vow.

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilled vow: The witnesses note the the days fulfilled: the term of separation completed. The witnesses note the brought to the door: coming to the tabernacle to discharge it. The witnesses note the before the LORD: the vow ended as an act of worship. The witnesses note the thanksgiving: a sacrifice for grace to keep the vow. The expositors observe the the days fulfilled: the term of separation completed. The expositors observe the brought to the door: coming to the tabernacle to discharge it. The expositors observe the before the LORD: the vow ended as an act of worship. The expositors observe the thanksgiving: a sacrifice for grace to keep the vow. The believer is taught to mark the the days fulfilled, the brought to the door, the before the LORD, the thanksgiving, and to be wholly consecrated to God. The verse thus records that the fulfilled vow was brought to the door of the tabernacle — the personal consecration concluded as a public act of worship and thanksgiving before the LORD.
14:
"And he shall offer his offering unto the LORD, one he lamb of the first year without blemish for a burnt offering, and one ewe lamb of the first year without blemish for a sin offering, and one ram without blemish for peace offerings," The witnesses note the threefold offering at the close of the vow — a lamb for a burnt offering, a ewe for a sin offering, and a ram for peace offerings. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Nazarite presented his offering at the door of the tabernacle, to the priest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch list the three victims, each without blemish. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a lamb for burnt offering — the gift of entire devotion; the a ewe for sin offering — owning the need of atonement; the a ram for peace offerings — fellowship and thanksgiving; the without blemish — each pointing to the unblemished Lamb.

The witnesses agree the finished vow was crowned with a full set of sacrifices: the burnt offering of entire devotion, the sin offering owning that even his holiest days had need of atonement, and the peace offering of fellowship and thanksgiving; the Nazarite, though consecrated, came at the last as a sinner needing cleansing and a worshipper rendering thanks — every offering pointing to the one Lamb without blemish in whom all are accepted.

So the witnesses make the verse the completing offerings: The witnesses note the a lamb for burnt offering: the gift of entire devotion. The witnesses note the a ewe for sin offering: owning the need of atonement. The witnesses note the a ram for peace offerings: fellowship and thanksgiving. The witnesses note the without blemish: each pointing to the unblemished Lamb. The expositors observe the a lamb for burnt offering: the gift of entire devotion. The expositors observe the a ewe for sin offering: owning the need of atonement. The expositors observe the a ram for peace offerings: fellowship and thanksgiving. The expositors observe the without blemish: each pointing to the unblemished Lamb. The believer is taught to mark the a lamb for burnt offering, the a ewe for sin offering, the a ram for peace offerings, the without blemish, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite closed his vow with burnt, sin, and peace offerings — the consecrated still needing atonement and rendering thanks, all pointing to the one Lamb without blemish.
15:
"And a basket of unleavened bread, cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, and wafers of unleavened bread anointed with oil, and their meat offering, and their drink offerings." The witnesses note the basket of unleavened bread and the accompanying meal and drink offerings brought with the sacrifices. The witnesses note that Gill notes the unleavened bread, as at the consecration of Aaron and his sons (Lev. 8:26), though for thanksgiving peace offerings leavened bread was offered. The witnesses note that Ellicott and Wesley note the meal and drink offerings that generally accompanied the sacrifices. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unleavened bread — the bread of sincerity, as at the priests' consecration; the mingled and anointed with oil — breathing of the Spirit; the meal and drink offerings — accompanying the sacrifices; the a full offering — the worship of the day complete.

The witnesses agree the bread and the accompaniments completed the worship of the day: the unleavened cakes mingled and anointed with oil, with the meal and drink offerings, were brought with the victims, that the offering be full and entire; the unleavened bread, the bread of sincerity, fitted the consecration now fulfilled, and the oil throughout breathed of the Spirit by whom alone such devotion is wrought and accepted.

So the witnesses make the verse the bread and accompaniments: The witnesses note the unleavened bread: the bread of sincerity, as at the priests' consecration. The witnesses note the mingled and anointed with oil: breathing of the Spirit. The witnesses note the meal and drink offerings: accompanying the sacrifices. The witnesses note the a full offering: the worship of the day complete. The expositors observe the unleavened bread: the bread of sincerity, as at the priests' consecration. The expositors observe the mingled and anointed with oil: breathing of the Spirit. The expositors observe the meal and drink offerings: accompanying the sacrifices. The expositors observe the a full offering: the worship of the day complete. The believer is taught to mark the unleavened bread, the mingled and anointed with oil, the meal and drink offerings, the a full offering, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite brought unleavened bread and the meal and drink offerings — the worship complete, the oil throughout breathing of the Spirit by whom devotion is wrought.
16:
"And the priest shall bring them before the LORD, and shall offer his sin offering, and his burnt offering:" The witnesses note the priest bringing all the offerings before the LORD and offering the sin and burnt offerings in order. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priest brought all the offerings to the altar and presented them to the LORD in the name of the Nazarite. The witnesses note that the sin offering was offered first, then the burnt offering, in the appointed order. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bring them before the LORD — the priest presenting all the offerings; the in the Nazarite's name — offered on the worshipper's behalf; the sin offering first — guilt put away before devotion; the through a mediator — the worship accepted by the priest.

The witnesses agree the priest stood between the Nazarite and God: it was not the worshipper but the priest who brought and offered the sacrifices, presenting them in the Nazarite's name; the order — sin offering first, then burnt — kept the gospel sequence, guilt put away before devotion is presented, and the whole done through a mediator, as the believer's worship is accepted only through the great High Priest.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's mediation: The witnesses note the bring them before the LORD: the priest presenting all the offerings. The witnesses note the in the Nazarite's name: offered on the worshipper's behalf. The witnesses note the sin offering first: guilt put away before devotion. The witnesses note the through a mediator: the worship accepted by the priest. The expositors observe the bring them before the LORD: the priest presenting all the offerings. The expositors observe the in the Nazarite's name: offered on the worshipper's behalf. The expositors observe the sin offering first: guilt put away before devotion. The expositors observe the through a mediator: the worship accepted by the priest. The believer is taught to mark the bring them before the LORD, the in the Nazarite's name, the sin offering first, the through a mediator, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the priest brought and offered the sacrifices in the Nazarite's name, sin offering first — guilt put away before devotion, the worship accepted through a mediator.
17:
"And he shall offer the ram for a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD, with the basket of unleavened bread: the priest shall offer also his meat offering, and his drink offering." The witnesses note the ram offered last as a peace offering, with the bread, meal, and drink offerings — the climax of the consecration. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ram was offered after the other two. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch see the completion of the consecration vow concentrated in the ram and the basket of unleavened bread for the peace offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the ram — offered last, the sacrifice of peace; the with the bread — the basket of unleavened bread; the the climax — the completion of the consecration vow; the ending in communion — guilt removed, devotion given, then fellowship.

The witnesses agree the peace offering crowned the whole: after sin was atoned and devotion presented, the ram of fellowship was offered with the bread, sealing the consecration in communion with God; the order led up to peace — guilt removed, life devoted, and then the feast of fellowship — a pattern of the gospel's own progress, ending in reconciled communion between God and his servant.

So the witnesses make the verse the peace offering: The witnesses note the the ram: offered last, the sacrifice of peace. The witnesses note the with the bread: the basket of unleavened bread. The witnesses note the the climax: the completion of the consecration vow. The witnesses note the ending in communion: guilt removed, devotion given, then fellowship. The expositors observe the the ram: offered last, the sacrifice of peace. The expositors observe the with the bread: the basket of unleavened bread. The expositors observe the the climax: the completion of the consecration vow. The expositors observe the ending in communion: guilt removed, devotion given, then fellowship. The believer is taught to mark the the ram, the with the bread, the the climax, the ending in communion, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the ram of peace offering crowned the consecration — the order ending in fellowship with God, a pattern of the gospel: guilt removed, life devoted, then reconciled communion.
18:
"And the Nazarite shall shave the head of his separation at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and shall take the hair of the head of his separation, and put it in the fire which is under the sacrifice of the peace offerings." The witnesses note the shaving of the consecrated hair, burned in the fire under the peace offering, the public sign that the vow was finished. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was done publicly at the door, that all might know the Nazariteship was at an end, and that the hair, consecrated to the Lord by the vow, was burned upon the altar fire. The witnesses note that the burning of the hair gave it wholly to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the shave the head — the consecrated hair cut at the door; the publicly — that all might know the vow was ended; the in the fire — burned under the peace offering; the given wholly to God — the badge of devotion itself offered up.

The witnesses agree the cutting and burning of the hair was the fitting close of the vow: the long locks, the very badge of the consecration, were not kept as a relic but offered up in the fire of the peace offering, given wholly to God; the public shaving at the tabernacle door declared the vow honourably fulfilled, and the burning rendered the token of devotion itself a kind of offering, the whole consecration consumed at last upon the altar.

So the witnesses make the verse the burned hair: The witnesses note the shave the head: the consecrated hair cut at the door. The witnesses note the publicly: that all might know the vow was ended. The witnesses note the in the fire: burned under the peace offering. The witnesses note the given wholly to God: the badge of devotion itself offered up. The expositors observe the shave the head: the consecrated hair cut at the door. The expositors observe the publicly: that all might know the vow was ended. The expositors observe the in the fire: burned under the peace offering. The expositors observe the given wholly to God: the badge of devotion itself offered up. The believer is taught to mark the shave the head, the publicly, the in the fire, the given wholly to God, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite shaved his consecrated hair and burned it under the peace offering — the badge of devotion itself offered up, the consecration consumed at last upon the altar.
19:
"And the priest shall take the sodden shoulder of the ram, and one unleavened cake out of the basket, and one unleavened wafer, and shall put them upon the hands of the Nazarite, after the hair of his separation is shaven:" The witnesses note the priest placing the boiled shoulder of the ram and the bread upon the Nazarite's hands after the shaving. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the left shoulder, the right being the priest's by another law, so that the priest here had both shoulders — a peculiar honour marking the sacredness of the vow. The witnesses note that the portions were put upon the Nazarite's hands after the hair was shaven. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sodden shoulder — the boiled shoulder of the ram; the the unleavened bread — a cake and a wafer with it; the the priest's double portion — both shoulders, marking the sacred vow; the upon the Nazarite's hands — his devotion presented to God.

The witnesses agree the special portion marked the special holiness of the vow: ordinarily the priest received but the right shoulder of a peace offering, yet from the Nazarite he had both, a greater gift answering to the greater consecration; the placing of the shoulder and bread on the Nazarite's hands made him, for the moment, a kind of offerer, his completed devotion acknowledged in this solemn presentation to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the portion presented: The witnesses note the the sodden shoulder: the boiled shoulder of the ram. The witnesses note the the unleavened bread: a cake and a wafer with it. The witnesses note the the priest's double portion: both shoulders, marking the sacred vow. The witnesses note the upon the Nazarite's hands: his devotion presented to God. The expositors observe the the sodden shoulder: the boiled shoulder of the ram. The expositors observe the the unleavened bread: a cake and a wafer with it. The expositors observe the the priest's double portion: both shoulders, marking the sacred vow. The expositors observe the upon the Nazarite's hands: his devotion presented to God. The believer is taught to mark the the sodden shoulder, the the unleavened bread, the the priest's double portion, the upon the Nazarite's hands, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the priest set the ram's shoulder and bread on the Nazarite's hands — the double portion marking the sacredness of the vow, his completed devotion presented to God.
20:
"And the priest shall wave them for a wave offering before the LORD... and after that the Nazarite may drink wine." The witnesses note the wave offering of the portions and the release of the Nazarite — after which he might drink wine again. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priest put his hands under the Nazarite's and waved them to and fro, testifying God's goodness, his sovereign dominion, and that all the Nazarite had and did was from him and through his help. The witnesses note that with this the vow was discharged and the Nazarite freed to drink wine. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the wave offering — the portions moved before the LORD; the acknowledging God — that all was from him and through his help; the the vow discharged — the separation honourably ended; the may drink wine — the Nazarite freed to common life again.

The witnesses agree the waving was a grateful acknowledgment: the portions moved before the LORD owned that all the Nazarite's ability to keep his vow had come from God, the offering a thanksgiving for grace given; and the closing word — "the Nazarite may drink wine" — shows the vow was for a season, not forever, the separation now honourably ended, the servant returning to common life having rendered his devotion in full.

So the witnesses make the verse the wave offering and release: The witnesses note the the wave offering: the portions moved before the LORD. The witnesses note the acknowledging God: that all was from him and through his help. The witnesses note the the vow discharged: the separation honourably ended. The witnesses note the may drink wine: the Nazarite freed to common life again. The expositors observe the the wave offering: the portions moved before the LORD. The expositors observe the acknowledging God: that all was from him and through his help. The expositors observe the the vow discharged: the separation honourably ended. The expositors observe the may drink wine: the Nazarite freed to common life again. The believer is taught to mark the the wave offering, the acknowledging God, the the vow discharged, the may drink wine, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the portions were waved before the LORD and the Nazarite released to drink wine — a thanksgiving that all was from God, the vow for a season honourably ended.
21:
"This is the law of the Nazarite who hath vowed, and of his offering unto the LORD for his separation, beside that that his hand shall get: according to the vow which he vowed, so he must do after the law of his separation." The witnesses note the summing of the Nazarite law — each to do according to his vow, beside whatever more his hand could give. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is what the Nazarite is obliged to do when his Nazariteship is up. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read him offering his sacrificial gifts according to the state of a Nazarite he had entered, beside what his means allowed him freely to add. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the law of the Nazarite — what he must do when the vow is up; the according to his vow — the appointed offerings exactly performed; the beside what his hand gets — free to add more as he is able; the a floor, not a ceiling — the vow paid in full, generosity welcome.

The witnesses agree the law fixed a floor but not a ceiling: the Nazarite must bring the appointed offerings, yet was free to give more as his hand could afford, his devotion measured by his vow but not bounded by it; the willing heart, having promised, was to perform exactly, and might add beyond — a pattern that what is vowed to God is to be paid in full, and generosity beyond the vow is welcome.

So the witnesses make the verse the summing of the law: The witnesses note the the law of the Nazarite: what he must do when the vow is up. The witnesses note the according to his vow: the appointed offerings exactly performed. The witnesses note the beside what his hand gets: free to add more as he is able. The witnesses note the a floor, not a ceiling: the vow paid in full, generosity welcome. The expositors observe the the law of the Nazarite: what he must do when the vow is up. The expositors observe the according to his vow: the appointed offerings exactly performed. The expositors observe the beside what his hand gets: free to add more as he is able. The expositors observe the a floor, not a ceiling: the vow paid in full, generosity welcome. The believer is taught to mark the the law of the Nazarite, the according to his vow, the beside what his hand gets, the a floor, not a ceiling, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the Nazarite did according to his vow, beside what more his hand could give — what is vowed to God paid in full, and generosity beyond it welcome.
22:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the law now turning to the priestly blessing, with which Aaron and his sons were to bless Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the connection some draw — that the Nazarites were holy persons, and so were the priests, the law of the one joined to the other. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch see the spiritual character of Israel culminating in the blessing with which the priests were to bless the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — god ordaining the priestly blessing; the after the Nazarite — the holy individual, then the blessed people; the the priests to bless — the benediction by their mouth; the Israel's spiritual height — the congregation's life culminating in the blessing.

The witnesses agree the placing lifts the chapter from the devotion of the individual to the blessing of the whole congregation: after the holiness of the Nazarite comes the benediction by which God would bless all his people through the priests; the spiritual life of Israel, the grammatical readers note, reached its height in this blessing — the LORD putting his own favour upon the nation by the mouth of his appointed ministers.

So the witnesses make the verse the turn to the blessing: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: God ordaining the priestly blessing. The witnesses note the after the Nazarite: the holy individual, then the blessed people. The witnesses note the the priests to bless: the benediction by their mouth. The witnesses note the Israel's spiritual height: the congregation's life culminating in the blessing. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: God ordaining the priestly blessing. The expositors observe the after the Nazarite: the holy individual, then the blessed people. The expositors observe the the priests to bless: the benediction by their mouth. The expositors observe the Israel's spiritual height: the congregation's life culminating in the blessing. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the after the Nazarite, the the priests to bless, the Israel's spiritual height, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that God now ordains the priestly blessing — the chapter rising from the Nazarite's holiness to the benediction of all Israel, the LORD's favour put upon his people by his ministers.
23:
"Speak unto Aaron and unto his sons, saying, On this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel, saying unto them," The witnesses note the charge given to Aaron and his sons — the form of words with which they were to bless Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes Aaron and his sons, who succeeded him in all generations, were the persons appointed publicly to bless the people. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the very words of the threefold blessing that follows. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron and his sons — the appointed blessers in every generation; the on this wise — the exact words divinely given; the not man's wish — the benediction God's own; the a ministry of grace — the priest the channel of God's favour.

The witnesses agree the blessing was no improvised wish but a fixed and divinely given form: the priests were to bless "on this wise," in the exact words the LORD appointed, that the benediction be God's own and not man's; the office of pronouncing blessing belonged to Aaron's line, a standing ministry of grace, the priest the appointed channel through whom God would speak his favour to his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointed form: The witnesses note the Aaron and his sons: the appointed blessers in every generation. The witnesses note the on this wise: the exact words divinely given. The witnesses note the not man's wish: the benediction God's own. The witnesses note the a ministry of grace: the priest the channel of God's favour. The expositors observe the Aaron and his sons: the appointed blessers in every generation. The expositors observe the on this wise: the exact words divinely given. The expositors observe the not man's wish: the benediction God's own. The expositors observe the a ministry of grace: the priest the channel of God's favour. The believer is taught to mark the Aaron and his sons, the on this wise, the not man's wish, the a ministry of grace, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that Aaron and his sons were to bless Israel in the appointed words — the benediction God's own and not man's, a standing ministry of grace through the priest.
24:
"The LORD bless thee, and keep thee:" The witnesses note the first clause of the blessing — the LORD bless and keep his people. The witnesses note that Gill sees in the threefold use of "Jehovah" through the blessing the three divine persons, Father, Son, and Spirit, each concerned in the blessing of the people of God. The witnesses note that the LORD's blessing is followed by his keeping — both the giving of good and the guarding of it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD bless thee — the giving of every good; the and keep thee — the guarding of what is given; the the threefold Jehovah — the Trinity foreshadowed in the blessing; the giving and preserving — the sum of all the believer needs.

The reformed reader (Gill) finds the Trinity foreshadowed in the threefold Jehovah, the Father blessing with every spiritual blessing, the Son with redeeming grace, the Spirit with sanctifying grace; and he marks the order — first "bless," then "keep," for God not only bestows good upon his people but preserves them in it, that what he gives be not lost; the blessing and the keeping together are the sum of all the believer needs.

So the witnesses make the verse bless and keep: The witnesses note the the LORD bless thee: the giving of every good. The witnesses note the and keep thee: the guarding of what is given. The witnesses note the the threefold Jehovah: the Trinity foreshadowed in the blessing. The witnesses note the giving and preserving: the sum of all the believer needs. The expositors observe the the LORD bless thee: the giving of every good. The expositors observe the and keep thee: the guarding of what is given. The expositors observe the the threefold Jehovah: the Trinity foreshadowed in the blessing. The expositors observe the giving and preserving: the sum of all the believer needs. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD bless thee, the and keep thee, the the threefold Jehovah, the giving and preserving, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the first clause prays the LORD to bless and keep — to give every good and guard it, Gill finding the Trinity in the threefold Jehovah, the sum of all the believer needs.
25:
"The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee:" The witnesses note the second clause — the LORD's face shining upon his people in grace. The witnesses note that Gill reads the shining face as God causing himself, the Sun of righteousness, to arise and shine, granting his gracious presence and the light of his countenance. The witnesses note that Wesley reads it of God's love made known — "the Lord love thee, and make thee know that he loves thee." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the make his face shine — the smile of God's favour; the the Sun of righteousness — his presence giving light and warmth; the be gracious — his free grace toward his people; the the sense of his love — the assurance that God loves us.

The witnesses agree the shining face is the smile of God's favour: as the sun rising enlightens and warms the earth, so the LORD's gracious presence gives light, warmth, and life to the soul; Wesley finds the heart of it in the assurance of God's love — that we cannot but be happy if we know that God loves us, the shining face being the felt sense of his grace and favour upon his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the shining face: The witnesses note the make his face shine: the smile of God's favour. The witnesses note the the Sun of righteousness: his presence giving light and warmth. The witnesses note the be gracious: his free grace toward his people. The witnesses note the the sense of his love: the assurance that God loves us. The expositors observe the make his face shine: the smile of God's favour. The expositors observe the the Sun of righteousness: his presence giving light and warmth. The expositors observe the be gracious: his free grace toward his people. The expositors observe the the sense of his love: the assurance that God loves us. The believer is taught to mark the make his face shine, the the Sun of righteousness, the be gracious, the the sense of his love, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the second clause prays the LORD's face to shine in grace — the smile of his favour, the felt assurance of his love, light and life to the soul.
26:
"The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace." The witnesses note the third clause — the LORD lifting up his countenance and giving peace, the crown of the blessing. The witnesses note that Gill reads the lifted countenance as God showing his face and favour, looking cheerfully on his people, declaring himself well pleased with them in Christ, and restoring the joys of his salvation. The witnesses note that the blessing ends in peace, the fruit of God's favour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the lift up his countenance — god's open, smiling favour; the well pleased in Christ — declaring his good pleasure in his people; the give thee peace — the crown of the whole blessing; the the climax — rising from blessing to peace.

The witnesses agree the climax of the blessing is peace: the LORD lifting up his countenance is his open, smiling favour, declaring himself well pleased with his people in Christ, and the gift that crowns all is peace — peace with God and the peace of God in the heart; the whole benediction rises from blessing and keeping, through the shining face of grace, to this last and fullest gift, the settled peace of those on whom God looks with favour.

So the witnesses make the verse the lifted countenance and peace: The witnesses note the lift up his countenance: God's open, smiling favour. The witnesses note the well pleased in Christ: declaring his good pleasure in his people. The witnesses note the give thee peace: the crown of the whole blessing. The witnesses note the the climax: rising from blessing to peace. The expositors observe the lift up his countenance: God's open, smiling favour. The expositors observe the well pleased in Christ: declaring his good pleasure in his people. The expositors observe the give thee peace: the crown of the whole blessing. The expositors observe the the climax: rising from blessing to peace. The believer is taught to mark the lift up his countenance, the well pleased in Christ, the give thee peace, the the climax, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the third clause prays the LORD to lift up his countenance and give peace — his smiling favour crowning the blessing with peace, the fullest gift to those on whom God looks with favour.
27:
"And they shall put my name upon the children of Israel; and I will bless them." The witnesses note the seal of the blessing — the priests putting God's name upon Israel, and God himself promising to bless them. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priests put God's name upon the people by calling them by his name and pronouncing the blessing in the name Jehovah, three times used in the form. The witnesses note that the LORD promises, "I will bless them" — really and truly, with blessings irreversible. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the put my name upon them — the priests pronouncing the blessing in God's name; the three times Jehovah — the name set thrice upon the people; the I will bless them — god himself making the blessing good; the irreversible blessings — real, substantial, and sure.

The witnesses agree the efficacy of the blessing lay not in the priests but in God: they did but put his name upon the people, pronouncing the words; it was the LORD himself who would make the blessing good, "I will bless them," with real and substantial and irreversible blessings; unless God blesses, in vain do the priests pronounce — so the benediction rests at last on the promise of God, who sets his name on his people and surely blesses those who bear it.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of the blessing: The witnesses note the put my name upon them: the priests pronouncing the blessing in God's name. The witnesses note the three times Jehovah: the name set thrice upon the people. The witnesses note the I will bless them: God himself making the blessing good. The witnesses note the irreversible blessings: real, substantial, and sure. The expositors observe the put my name upon them: the priests pronouncing the blessing in God's name. The expositors observe the three times Jehovah: the name set thrice upon the people. The expositors observe the I will bless them: God himself making the blessing good. The expositors observe the irreversible blessings: real, substantial, and sure. The believer is taught to mark the put my name upon them, the three times Jehovah, the I will bless them, the irreversible blessings, and to rest under the blessing of God. The verse thus records that the priests put God's name upon Israel and God promised "I will bless them" — the blessing resting at last on God himself, who sets his name on his people and surely blesses those who bear it.
